whatItDoes: Calculates IPv4 subnet details from CIDR notation, subnet mask, or slash-mask input. Displays network address, broadcast address, subnet mask, wildcard mask, usable host range, total and usable host counts, and a binary representation of the network address and mask.
whyItExists: Manually computing subnet boundaries, host ranges, and broadcast addresses is tedious and error-prone. This tool accepts multiple input formats (CIDR, subnet mask, or slash-mask) and instantly returns all critical subnet details with a binary visualization.
whoShouldUse: Network administrators, DevOps engineers configuring cloud VPCs and container networks, and students learning IP subnetting fundamentals.
useCases: Calculate the network and broadcast addresses for a new /24 subnet before assigning static IPs
Convert a subnet mask like 255.255.255.192 to its CIDR equivalent and see the resulting host range
Verify that two IP addresses fall within the same subnet by comparing network addresses
Plan IP allocations for a multi-tier deployment by checking usable host counts at different prefix lengths
instructions: Enter an IPv4 address with CIDR notation (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24), a subnet mask (e.g., 192.168.1.0 255.255.255.0), or a slash-mask (e.g., 192.168.1.0/255.255.255.0)
Press Calculate or hit Enter to compute subnet details
Review the network address, broadcast address, subnet mask, wildcard mask, and usable host range
Check the binary view to visually verify the network and host portions of the address
examples: Input: '10.0.0.0 255.255.0.0' — Output: Network: 10.0.0.0, Broadcast: 10.0.255.255, Subnet Mask: 255.255.0.0, CIDR: /16, Usable Hosts: 65,534
bestPractices: Always verify the calculated network address matches your expected subnet boundary
Remember that the network and broadcast addresses are not assignable to hosts
For cloud VPCs, account for the 3-5 reserved IPs per subnet that providers like AWS and GCP hold
commonMistakes: Using an invalid subnet mask that does not align on byte boundaries (e.g., 255.255.255.130 is invalid)
Confusing the wildcard mask with the subnet mask — wildcard is the bitwise inverse
Forgetting that /31 and /32 subnets have special host count rules
faq: What input formats are supported? — CIDR notation (192.168.1.0/24), IP + subnet mask (192.168.1.0 255.255.255.0), or IP/slash-mask (192.168.1.0/255.255.255.0).
What is a wildcard mask? — It is the bitwise inverse of the subnet mask, commonly used in ACLs and routing protocols.
Why does /31 show a different host range? — /31 subnets use point-to-point conventions where both addresses can be assigned to hosts.
